reforms.description: The Government has modified its policies regarding R&D and innovation to improve the efficiency of publicly-funded laboratories and better direct their efforts towards industry needs, while also supporting co-operative pre-competitive research in key fields. Additionally, the Australian government has introduced a delayed removal of concessions for research and development expenditure, allowing time to address market imperfections that may have led to under-investment in R&D activities. Furthermore, the Commonwealth Government has restructured assistance to apprenticeships, moving from direct wage subsidies to incentives aimed at maintaining high levels of apprenticeship recruitment and improving training quality.
reforms.component_name: Public Laboratory Efficiency and Apprenticeship Restructuring
reforms.growth_orientation_rationale: The reforms aim to enhance R&D efficiency and address market imperfections, which are expected to stimulate private sector investment and improve workforce skills.
reforms.status_evidence: The reforms have been put into practice as indicated by their implementation status.
reforms.importance_rationale: While the reforms are significant in scope, they do not represent a structural overhaul but rather adjustments to existing policies.
reforms.source_quote: Assistance to apprenticeships (CRAFT) has recently been restructured. Direct wage subsidies have been abandoned in favour of incentives to maintain a high level of apprenticeship recruitment, to complete training and to improve its quality.

reforms.description: The Government introduced a 150 per cent R&D expenditure deduction against tax in 1985 to promote increased private sector R&D spending, which will be reduced to 100 per cent from July 1991.
reforms.growth_orientation_rationale: The tax deduction is designed to incentivize private sector investment in R&D, which is expected to enhance innovation and productivity.
reforms.status_evidence: The reform was introduced in 1985 and is set to change in 1991.
reforms.importance_rationale: The introduction of a significant tax incentive for R&D represents a major reform in the funding landscape for private sector innovation.
reforms.source_quote: The Government introduced in 1985 the 150 per cent R&D expenditure deduction against tax to promote increased private sector R&D spending.

reforms.description: The Australian Traineeship System (ATS) was introduced to provide training for young people in the non-trades area, specifically targeting early school leavers transitioning to the workforce.
reforms.growth_orientation_rationale: The ATS aims to enhance the skills of young people, thereby improving their employability and contributing to the overall skill level of the workforce.
reforms.status_evidence: The ATS has been put into practice as indicated by its implementation status.
reforms.importance_rationale: The introduction of the ATS is significant for workforce development but does not represent a major overhaul of the training system.
reforms.source_quote: The Australian Traineeship System (ATS), introduced in 1985 with a view to providing training for young people in the non-trades area, is specifically aimed at early school leavers making their transition to the workforce.

reforms.description: The Youth Training Programme (YTP) and Adult Training Programme (ATP) were established to provide training opportunities for disadvantaged job seekers, facilitating their entry into approved courses.
reforms.growth_orientation_rationale: These programmes aim to enhance the skills of disadvantaged job seekers, thereby improving their employability and contributing to the overall skill level of the workforce.
reforms.status_evidence: The YTP and ATP have been put into practice as indicated by their implementation status.
reforms.importance_rationale: The establishment of the YTP and ATP is significant for workforce development but does not represent a major overhaul of the training system.
reforms.source_quote: The Youth Training Programme (YTP), and Adult Training Programme (ATP) are both mainly targeted at disadvantaged job seekers who have been unemployed for six months or more, providing entrance into approved courses either through the TAFE system or using private or industry-based training.
